title: Nanoscale Sensing of Solid-State Samples with High Frequency Resolution
authors: P. Alsina-Bolívar, D. B. Bucher, J. Casanova
abstract: To meet the growing demand for nanoscale surface analysis, nitrogen-vacancy (NV) centers offer a high-sensitivity alternative by leveraging their ability to operate in immediate proximity to the sample. In this work, we propose a quantum control protocol designed to overcome the inherent challenges of solid-state environments, specifically by mitigating anisotropy and strong dipole-dipole interactions to enable the detection of isotropic chemical shifts at the nanoscale. To achieve this, our scheme synchronizes a slowly rotating magnetic field with tailored RF decoupling and MW control of the NV sensors. We provide an analytical mapping that explicitly links the measured spectrum to the control sequence features and the underlying system parameters, enabling a straightforward characterization of the sample.
summary: This paper presents a new quantum control method for using NV centers to perform high-resolution NMR on solid-state surfaces. By synchronizing a rotating magnetic field with specific RF decoupling pulses, the researchers can suppress unwanted anisotropic interactions and dipolar couplings. This allows for the precise detection of isotropic chemical shifts, which serve as a unique fingerprint for nanoscale materials. The study demonstrates that the protocol is robust against experimental imperfections like magnetic field misalignment and amplitude noise.
main_problem: Achieving high-frequency resolution in nanoscale NMR spectroscopy using NV centers is hindered by chemical shift anisotropy (CSA) and strong dipole-dipole interactions in solid-state samples.
main_result: The authors propose a quantum control protocol using a rotating magnetic field and RF decoupling that successfully isolates the isotropic chemical shift and provides a robust analytical mapping for sample characterization.
method: The protocol employs a slowly rotating magnetic field to average out CSA and a frequency-switched Lee-Goldburg (fsLG) inspired RF scheme for dipolar decoupling, combined with multi-point correlation spectroscopy.
model_or_system: The system consists of an ensemble of NV centers in diamond (electron and nitrogen memory spins) acting as sensors to probe a solid-state sample containing a collection of nuclear spins (e.g., protons) in the statistical polarization regime.
key_observables: Isotropic chemical shifts (delta_iso), Bloch-Siegert shifts, and the averaged electron spin state (S_z).
important_parameters: Magnetic field rotation frequency (nu), RF Rabi frequency (Omega), magnetic field aperture (epsilon), and the number of spins (N).
main_assumptions: The rotation frequency is much smaller than the Zeeman splitting; the effective Rabi frequency is the dominant interaction; and the large N limit allows approximating nuclear spin operators with expectation values.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the NV sensor schematic, magnetization evolution, and the rotating magnetic field trajectory. Figure 2 presents simulated NMR spectra for non-equivalent protons, demonstrating robustness against misalignment and noise.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the challenge of nanoscale sensing, describes the physical model and Hamiltonian, details the proposed rotating field and RF control protocol, provides an analytical derivation of the effective Hamiltonian using Average Hamiltonian Theory, and validates the method through numerical simulations of error robustness.
why_interesting: The paper presents an advanced quantum control protocol for managing many-body interactions (dipolar coupling) and anisotropy, which is highly relevant to the control of spin ensembles and the development of high-precision quantum sensors.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. a) (Top) Each sensor comprising an electron and a memory qubit, probes a distinct region of the sample (for clarity, only the j-th and ( j + 1)-th units in the ensemble are depicted). These regions exhibit distinct initial magnetizations ( ⃗M j and ⃗ M j+1) as a result of statistical polarisation. (Bottom) After a time τ each magnetization vector evolves owing to the combined action of ⃗B0(t) and Hc(t) in Eqs. (2) and (3). b) Trajectory of ⃗B0(t) in Eq. (2). This traces a cone, with aperture ϵ and central axis ˆn. The inset shows in more detail the diamond-sample block irradiated with RF and MWs. c) (Top) Signal emitted by the sample. Figure 2. Simulated spectrum for a sample with two magnetically non-equivalent protons. The solid colored lines represent the spec- tra obtained using our protocol under various misalignment errors, while gray crosses depict the typical powder spectrum (for a non- rotating magnetic field) for comparison. Dashed vertical lines in- dicate the theoretical resonances predicted by our framework. The results demonstrate the protocol’s robustness against both increas- ing misalignments and stochastic amplitude noise (0.25% fluctua- tions with a 1 ms correlation time).
